children.name: Hydrochoerus
children.description: Capybaras — the world's largest rodents, semi-aquatic grazers of South American rivers, lakes, and marshes. A large male can weigh 66 kg. Capybaras are highly social, living in groups of 10–20 that may swell to hundreds during the dry season around shrinking water bodies. They are excellent swimmers and can remain submerged for up to five minutes to evade predators. Their eyes, ears, and nostrils are positioned high on the head, allowing them to monitor their surroundings while almost completely submerged. Jaguars, anacondas, caimans, pumas, and harpy eagles all prey on capybaras, making them a keystone prey species across South American wetlands.

children.name: Hydrochoerus hydrochaeris
children.description: The world's largest rodent — up to 1.3 m long and 66 kg. Highly social; their calm temperament and photogenic sociability has made them internet icons. In Venezuela they are farmed for meat under special Catholic dispensations that classify them as fish during Lent.

children.name: Hydrochoerus isthmius
children.description: A smaller relative of the capybara found from Panama to western Colombia and Venezuela. Once considered a subspecies, it is now recognised as a distinct species. Smaller and with a more restricted range, inhabiting lowland wetlands and riverine forests.

children.name: Cavia
children.description: Guinea pigs — small, stout rodents of the Andes, domesticated by Andean peoples at least 5,000 years ago for food and still a dietary staple in Peru, Bolivia, and Ecuador. Guinea pigs (called 'cuy') are more efficient at converting plant matter to protein than cattle, and they produce no greenhouse gases. Domestic guinea pigs (Cavia porcellus) descend from wild species still found in the Andes and differ significantly from their wild ancestors in coat colour, body size, and behaviour. Wild guinea pigs are shy and cryptic; domestic ones are placid and tolerant of handling.

children.name: Cavia porcellus
children.description: Domesticated for at least 5,000 years; no wild populations exist. A critical model organism in immunology and nutrition research — 'guinea pig' became synonymous with experimental subject. Highly vocal with a rich repertoire of purrs, rumbles, and squeaks.

children.name: Cavia aperea
children.description: The wild species most closely related to the domestic guinea pig. Lives in dense grassland and forest edge. Much more skittish than domestic animals, fleeing at the slightest disturbance.

children.name: Cavia tschudii
children.description: Found in the Andes from Peru to northwestern Argentina at elevations up to 4,000 m. Considered the closest wild relative of the domestic guinea pig by some studies. Inhabits rocky outcrops and bunchgrass.

children.name: Cavia fulgida
children.description: A medium-sized guinea pig from eastern Brazil, found in the Atlantic forest and cerrado regions. Distinguished by its glossy, reddish-brown coat. Relatively poorly studied compared to related species.

children.name: Cavia magna
children.description: The largest wild Cavia, found in coastal marshes and grasslands of Uruguay and extreme southern Brazil. Semi-aquatic, spending much of its time in wet grasslands and marshes. Often found in small family groups.

children.name: Cavia intermedia
children.description: One of the most range-restricted rodents in the world, found only on Moleques do Sul Island off the coast of Brazil. With a total island area of just 12 hectares, it has the smallest known distribution of any caviid. Listed as Critically Endangered.

children.name: Dolichotis
children.description: Maras (Patagonian cavies) — large, long-legged guinea pig relatives that are among the most unusual rodents on Earth. With their long ears, hare-like legs, and swift bounding gait, they bear a striking resemblance to small deer or jackrabbits, a classic example of convergent evolution. Maras are unusual among rodents in forming long-term monogamous pairs; a male will follow his female closely and drive away rival males. They use a communal crèche system where multiple pairs deposit their young in a shared burrow tended by a rotating group of adults.

children.name: Dolichotis patagonum
children.description: Can reach 45 cm at the shoulder and weigh 9 kg — the fourth largest rodent. Runs at up to 45 km/h. Population declining due to livestock grazing, habitat loss, and hunting; near threatened.

children.name: Dolichotis salinicola
children.description: A smaller mara found in the dry Gran Chaco of Paraguay, Bolivia, and Argentina. Also known as the salt desert cavy. More arid-adapted than the Patagonian mara, inhabiting scrubland and thorn forest.

children.name: Kerodon
children.description: Rock cavies — unique among caviids for their arboreal and rock- dwelling habits, found in the dry, rocky outcrops (lajeiros) of the Brazilian caatinga and cerrado. They are agile climbers, leaping confidently among boulders with the help of small, padded feet. Unlike most caviids, rock cavies are not grazers but specialized browsers on leaves, bark, and seeds.

children.name: Kerodon rupestris
children.description: Endemic to the dry, rocky caatinga of northeastern Brazil. An agile climber on granite outcrops. Eats bark, leaves, and seeds. One of the few caviids that can tolerate prolonged heat and aridity.

children.name: Kerodon acrobata
children.description: A recently discovered species from the cerrado and caatinga of central Brazil. Even more agile than its relative, capable of climbing trees as well as rocks. Its prehensile-like climbing abilities are unique among caviids.

children.name: Galea
children.description: Yellow-toothed cavies — smaller, more gracile relatives of guinea pigs with distinctive yellow-orange enamel on their incisors. They inhabit open grasslands, scrub, and rocky slopes across a wide range of South America, from the Andes to the Atlantic coast. Socially they are unusual among caviids: they live in groups with a dominance hierarchy but lack the strong monogamous pair bonds seen in maras.

children.name: Galea musteloides
children.description: Widely distributed in southern South America, from Peru and Bolivia through Chile and Argentina. Inhabits dry grasslands and scrub. Has yellow-orange incisors. Often found in colonial burrow systems in loose soil.

children.name: Galea spixii
children.description: Found in eastern Brazil, from the caatinga to the cerrado. Named after German naturalist Johann Baptist von Spix. Unlike other Galea species, it can inhabit both dry and humid habitats.

children.name: Galea flavidens
children.description: Restricted to central and eastern Brazil, favouring rocky cerrado habitats. Named for its bright yellow incisors, the most vividly coloured in the genus. Poorly known compared to its congeners.

children.name: Galea leucoblephara
children.description: Found in lowland grasslands and marshes of Uruguay, Argentina, and extreme southern Brazil. Distinguished by a white eye-ring. Adapted to wetter environments than other Galea species.

children.name: Microcavia
children.description: Mountain cavies — the smallest members of the guinea pig family, adapted to arid and high-altitude environments in the southern Andes and Patagonia. They are social, diurnal rodents that live in burrow systems and feed on grasses, leaves, and seeds. Their compact size and efficient water metabolism allow them to thrive in some of the driest habitats on the continent.

children.name: Microcavia australis
children.description: Widely distributed from the Andean foothills of northwestern Argentina to the arid scrub of Patagonia. Highly social, living in colonies of up to 50 individuals. Digs extensive burrow systems in sandy soil.

children.name: Microcavia niata
children.description: A high-altitude specialist of the central Andes in Peru, Bolivia, Chile, and Argentina, found above 3,000 m. Its dense, soft fur provides insulation in the cold, dry altiplano environment.
